scale_note: Score each speech ONLY from its own text. Use coarse values (integers or .5), never false precision. If the speech does not speak to a dimension, use null for that dimension (do NOT guess 0). Hawkish = leans toward tighter policy / more worried about inflation; dovish = leans toward easier policy / more worried about employment.

dimensions.label: Hawk-dove composite

anchors.-5: strongly dovish (wants cuts now / very worried about labor)
anchors.5: strongly hawkish (wants holds-or-hikes / very worried about inflation)

dimensions.guide: Overall lean of THIS speech's monetary-policy message. The headline number.

dimensions.label: Mandate weighting

anchors.-5: almost all weight on employment / max-employment side
anchors.5: almost all weight on price stability / inflation

dimensions.guide: Which side of the dual mandate the speaker emphasizes as the binding concern.

dimensions.label: Inflation conviction

anchors.0: confident disinflation is essentially done / on track
anchors.5: worried inflation is persistent / re-accelerating / tariff-driven upside

dimensions.guide: How worried the speaker is that inflation will NOT return to 2% on its own.

dimensions.label: Labor-market concern

anchors.5: alarmed about labor-market deterioration / downside risk to jobs

dimensions.guide: How worried the speaker is about a weakening labor market.

dimensions.label: Policy-stance read

anchors.0: policy is at/near neutral or even loose; little room/need to keep it tight
anchors.5: policy is clearly restrictive and should stay restrictive

dimensions.guide: How restrictive the speaker judges the CURRENT stance to be (and implicitly how much room to cut).

near_term_bias.label: Near-term policy bias
near_term_bias.guide: The speaker's near-term inclination for the next move(s) and the pace they signal.

theme_flags.label: Idiosyncratic themes
theme_flags.guide: Tag the policy-relevant themes the speaker leans on. Empty list if none stand out.

drivers.label: Drivers of the policy stance
drivers.guide: Decompose WHY the speaker holds the stance they do IN THIS SPEECH. For each driver give an `intensity` 0-3 (0 = not a factor / not mentioned; 1 = mentioned; 2 = important; 3 = central to their policy argument) and a `push` in {hawkish, dovish, neutral} = which way this factor pushes THEIR policy lean. Example: an oil/war supply shock a speaker fears will raise inflation persistently -> oil_war intensity 3, push hawkish; a labor market they see as stable-but-not-booming and no longer a reason to cut -> labor_market intensity 2, push neutral. This is the heart of the 'why are they turning hawkish/dovish' decomposition, so be precise and ground each in the text.

items.label: Oil / war (supply shock)
items.covers: energy/oil prices, the Middle East conflict, geopolitical or other supply-side cost shocks, input-cost pressure from energy

items.label: Underlying inflation / broadening
items.covers: core/underlying inflation persistence and stickiness, BROADENING/diffusion across categories, services ex-housing, inflation expectations becoming unanchored, the cumulative >2% overshoot

items.label: Tariffs
items.covers: tariff-driven goods-price pressure, whether to look through it, pass-through

items.label: Labor market
items.covers: labor tightness/looseness; weakening jobs (dovish push) vs solid/tight labor or low unemployment removing the case to ease (hawkish/neutral push)

items.label: Activity / demand
items.covers: GDP/consumer-spending strength (hawkish) or weakness/recession risk (dovish), AI investment boom

items.label: Other (FCI, fiscal, r*, independence)
items.covers: financial conditions/stability, fiscal/debt, the level of r*, productivity as a rate driver, Fed-independence considerations, the current restrictive policy setting itself

non_policy_rule: Set non_policy=true when the speech is essentially about bank regulation, payments/stablecoins, community development, ceremonial/acceptance remarks, or operations, with no substantive read on the macro outlook or the stance of monetary policy. Such speeches are kept in the corpus but down-weighted in the reaction-function aggregation.
summary_rule: summary = one tight paragraph (3-5 sentences), plain English, focused on the speaker's MONETARY-POLICY message (their read of inflation, labor, the stance, and where they lean). Not a generic abstract.
quotes_rule: key_quotes = 2-4 VERBATIM sentences from the speech that best reveal the reaction function, each tagged with the dimension it speaks to and a short surrounding-context note so it is auditable. Do not paraphrase inside quote text.
